revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:東遊]]
'''Azuma-asobi''' (東遊) is a long {{ill|Suite (music)|en|Suite (music)|qid=Q203005|lt=suite|1=Suite (music)}} categorized under the {{ill|Gagaku|en|Gagaku|qid=Q718540|lt=Gagaku|1=Gagaku}} genre of native Japanese music and dance. == Overview == Azuma-asobi is a relatively lengthy suite, requiring approximately 30 minutes for a complete performance. It is danced to {{ill|folk songs|ja|風俗歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/folk songs|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} originating from the {{ill|Tōgoku|ja|東国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōgoku|1=Tōgoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10343838}} (Eastern Provinces), corresponding to the Yamato Uta of the Yamato region. The dancers number six or four, and the accompanying musicians perform using {{ill|Metre (music)|en|Metre (music)|qid=Q630649|lt=Hyōshi|1=Metre (music)}}, {{ill|Yamatogoto|en|Yamatogoto|qid=Q3571466|lt=Wagoto|1=Yamatogoto}}, {{ill|Koto-mochi|ja|琴持|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koto-mochi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Azuma-asobi flute|ja|東遊笛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma-asobi flute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (chūkan), {{ill|Hichiriki|en|Hichiriki|qid=Q425748|lt=Hichiriki|1=Hichiriki}}, and {{ill|Tsukauta|ja|付歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukauta|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, in modern performances outside the {{ill|Music Department of the Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁式部職楽部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Music Department of the Imperial Household Agency|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内庁式部職|1=Board of Ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q98687444}}, the {{ill|Komabue|en|Komabue|qid=Q1064880|lt=Koma-bue|1=Komabue}} often substitutes the Azuma-asobi flute. Azuma-asobi is primarily performed as a {{ill|sacred dance|ja|神事舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sacred dance|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and has been employed in court rituals such as the {{ill|Emperor Jimmu Festival|ja|神武天皇祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu Tennō Festival|qq=}} on April 3, the spring and autumn {{ill|Kōreisai|en|Kōreisai|qid=Q6455253|lt=Kōrei Festival|1=Kōreisai}}, and at select shrines like {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|qid=Q714559|lt=Kasuga-taisha|1=Kasuga-taisha}}, {{ill|Kamo shrines|en|Kamo shrines|qid=Q1306606|lt=Kamo Shrine|1=Kamo shrines}}, and {{ill|Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine|ja|大宮氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社|1=Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q703633}} since the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}. According to its origin legend, Azuma-asobi replicates the dance of a {{ill|celestial maiden|ja|天女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/celestial maiden|qq=}} who descended to {{ill|Utobama|ja|宇土浜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utobama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|qid=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}} (present-day {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|qid=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}) during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ankan|en|Emperor Ankan|qid=Q241765|lt=Emperor Ankan|1=Emperor Ankan}}. The earliest literary mention appears in the ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|qid=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}'', where the ''Tōgoku no Gaku'' (Eastern Country Music) mentioned in the entry for {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|qid=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji 7|1=Tenpyō-hōji}} (763), January 17, is considered its precursor. The term "Azuma-asobi" first appears in ''{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|en|Nihon Kiryaku|qid=Q1053542|lt=Nihon Kiryaku|1=Nihon Kiryaku}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Tengyō|en|Tengyō|qid=Q1327340|lt=Tenkei 5|1=Tengyō}} (942), June 21, describing "the performance of Azuma-asobi horseback racing in ten rows at Gion Shrine." The performance of two dances, the Suruga-mai and Motomego-no-uta, is called ''Moromai,'' while performing only the Suruga-mai is referred to as ''Katamai.'' == History == During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, {{ill|court dances|ja|舞曲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court dances|1=dance|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8195422}} like Azuma-asobi were typically performed by {{ill|konoe|ja|近衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/konoe|1=Konoe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q440208}} officials. Dancers wore crowns with {{ill|Cherry blossom|en|Cherry blossom|qid=Q871991|lt=sakura|1=Cherry blossom}} inserted on the right, paired with white ritual robes (''kimi'') and swords. The dance was performed in groups of four or six, with singers accompanied by {{ill|Shakubyoshi|en|Shakubyoshi|qid=Q20983120|lt=shakubyōshi|1=Shakubyoshi}}, {{ill|koma-bue|ja|狛笛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/koma-bue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hichiriki|en|Hichiriki|qid=Q425748|lt=hichiriki|1=Hichiriki}}, and {{ill|Yamatogoto|en|Yamatogoto|qid=Q3571466|lt=wagoto|1=Yamatogoto}}. The dances began with the Suruga-mai and transitioned to the Motomego-mai, where dancers performed with flowing sleeves, either facing or back-to-back in a circular formation. Although Azuma-asobi predates {{ill|Saibara|en|Saibara|qid=Q1428885|lt=Saibara|1=Saibara}}, it originated in the {{ill|Eastern Provinces|ja|東国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Provinces|1=Tōgoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10343838}} and was later introduced to the {{ill|capital|ja|都|lt=capital|1=metropolitan prefecture|3=都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|13=User:Immanuelle/Metropolitan prefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q124313007}} as foreign music became popular. It was already in decline by {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|qid=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan 3|1=Zhenguan}} (861), when Azuma-asobi was performed during the {{ill|Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺大仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Buddha of Tōdai-ji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東大寺盧舎那仏像|1=Great Buddha of Tōdaiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11526469}} ceremony along with {{ill|Tōgaku|en|Tōgaku|qid=Q8011200|lt=Tang music|1=Tōgaku}}, {{ill|Komagaku|en|Komagaku|qid=Q693322|lt=Koma music|1=Komagaku}}, and {{ill|Rin'yū music|ja|林邑楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rin'yū music|1=Rin'yūgaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q65271568}}. In {{ill|Kanpyō (era)|en|Kanpyō (era)|qid=Q1346292|lt=Kanpyō|1=Kanpyō (era)}} (889), Azuma-asobi was incorporated into the {{ill|Kamo Festival|ja|賀茂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 葵祭|1=Aoi Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q773085}}, transitioning from a regional folk performance to a central ritual dance. In {{ill|Tengyō|en|Tengyō|qid=Q1327340|lt=Tenkei 5|1=Tengyō}} (942), Azuma-asobi was performed during the rites at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja|石清水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwashimizu Hachimangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|qid=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}} (r. {{ill|Kanna (era)|en|Kanna (era)|qid=Q1327053|lt=Kanna 2|1=Kanna (era)}} (986) - {{ill|Kankō|en|Kankō|qid=Q2736722|lt=Kankō 8|1=Kankō}} (1011)) sought to preserve the vanishing Kagura and formalized five Azuma-asobi songs: Ichika, Nika, Suruga-uta, Motomego-uta, and Kataoroshi (also known as Ōhiro-uta). This standardization is referenced in ancient scores such as the Tenji manuscripts, where an imperial decree in {{ill|Engi (era)|en|Engi (era)|qid=Q1374325|lt=Engi 20|1=Engi (era)}} (921) is mentioned. The scarcity of lyrics is attributed to Azuma-asobi’s adoption in rituals, which altered its original singing style. Though it disappeared from court for a time, Azuma-asobi was revived and adapted during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}.<ref>Takayama (2004)</ref> Since the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, Azuma-asobi has been performed at the {{ill|Emperor Jimmu Festival|ja|神武天皇祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu Tennō Festival|qq=}} and the spring and autumn {{ill|Kōreisai|en|Kōreisai|qid=Q6455253|lt=Kōrei Festival|1=Kōreisai}} by the Gagaku musicians in front of the {{ill|Kōreiden|ja|皇霊殿|lt=Kōrei-den (Imperial Ancestral Shrines)|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōreiden|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮中三殿}}. == Contents == The suite consists of five sections: {{ill|Ichika|ja|一歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichika|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nika|ja|二歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nika|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Suruga-uta|ja|駿河歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suruga-uta|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Motomego-uta|ja|求子歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motomego-uta|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Ōhiro-uta|ja|大比礼歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiro-uta|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Each section begins with a solo sung by the ''kutō'' (main vocalist) and transitions to a chorus joined by other singers (''tsuke-uta,'' currently two). Instruments include the {{ill|Shakubyoshi|en|Shakubyoshi|qid=Q20983120|lt=shakubyōshi|1=Shakubyoshi}} for rhythm, as well as the {{ill|Hichiriki|en|Hichiriki|qid=Q425748|lt=hichiriki|1=Hichiriki}}, {{ill|Komabue|en|Komabue|qid=Q1064880|lt=Koma-bue|1=Komabue}}, and {{ill|Yamatogoto|en|Yamatogoto|qid=Q3571466|lt=wagoto|1=Yamatogoto}}. Accompanying musicians, known as ''kotomochi,'' hold the wagoto during performances. Preludes and interludes, either instrumental or vocal, are integrated into the songs. The suite alternates between rhythmic patterns: structured rhythms (''agabyōshi'') and free rhythms (''shizubyōshi''). The ''Suruga-mai'' (Suruga dance) and ''Motomego-mai'' (Motomego dance) feature accompanying choreography. ; Performance Segments and Lyrics: 1. {{ill|Koma-jōshi|ja|狛調子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koma-jōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (instruments: flute, hichiriki, wagoto) – Free rhythm 2. {{ill|Aha-re|ja|阿波礼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aha-re|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (choral singing; instruments: wagoto, shakubyōshi) – Free rhythm "Tenpare (Ahare), o o o o" 3. {{ill|Kowadashi|ja|音出|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kowadashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (instruments: flute, hichiriki) – Free rhythm 4. {{ill|Oburi|ja|於振|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oburi|1=Ofuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11504398}} (choral singing; instruments: wagoto, shakubyōshi) – Free rhythm ... == Notes == {{Reflist|qq=}} == References == * {{ill|Takayama Shigeru|ja|高山茂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayama Shigeru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, "Azuma-asobi," in ''Nippon Daihyakka Zensho'' (Super Nipponica Professional Win Edition), Shogakukan, 2004. ISBN 4099067459. {{日本の音楽|qq=}} {{雅楽|qq=}} {{japan-culture-stub|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|東遊|version=94324962|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:東遊]]
